examples.title: Forward Chaining Algorithm
examples.description: Basic pseudocode for forward chaining inference
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Forward_Chaining
BEGIN
 WorkingMemory ← InitialFacts
 REPEAT
 MatchingRules ← {} // Rules whose conditions are satisfied
 FOR EACH rule IN RuleBase DO
 IF rule.condition ⊆ WorkingMemory THEN
 ADD rule TO MatchingRules
 END IF END FOR // Match phase IF MatchingRules IS EMPTY THEN BREAK // No rules can fire END IF SelectedRule ← Conflict_Resolution(MatchingRules) // Select phase Execute(SelectedRule) // Execute phase ADD SelectedRule.conclusion TO WorkingMemory UNTIL Goal_Reached OR No_More_Rules RETURN WorkingMemory
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm starts with initial facts in working memory, repeatedly matches rules against facts, resolves conflicts when multiple rules match, executes the selected rule to add new facts, and continues until goal is reached or no rules can fire.

examples.title: Conflict Resolution Strategies
examples.description: Implementation of common conflict resolution heuristics
examples.code:
FUNCTION Conflict_Resolution(MatchingRules)
BEGIN
 // Strategy 1: Recency FOR EACH rule IN MatchingRules DO rule.score ← Get_Recency_Score(rule) END FOR // Strategy 2: Specificity // (can combine multiple strategies) bestRule ← MatchingRules[0] FOR i ← 1 TO MatchingRules.length-1 DO IF MatchingRules[i].score > bestRule.score THEN bestRule ← MatchingRules[i] END IF END FOR RETURN bestRule
END

FUNCTION Get_Recency_Score(rule)
BEGIN score ← 0 FOR EACH condition IN rule.conditions DO factIndex ← Find_Fact_In_WM(condition) score ← score + (WM_Size - factIndex) END FOR RETURN score
END
examples.explanation: Recency assigns higher scores to rules using facts more recently added to working memory. Specificity would add bonus points for each condition in the rule. Multiple strategies can be combined with weights.

examples.title: Rule Firing Example
examples.description: Demonstrates rule firing with working memory updates
examples.explanation: This example shows the chaining effect where each rule's conclusion enables another rule to fire. Starting with facts X and Y, we derive Z, then W, then V through sequential rule firing.
